claim_verdicts.slide: What AI assistants do well (green zone)
claim_verdicts.bullet: Summarising text is a high-reliability task for current chat tools — LLM summaries consistently match or beat human-written ones
claim_verdicts.source_excerpt: Summaries generated by the LLMs consistently outperform both human and summaries generated by fine-tuned models across all tasks.
claim_verdicts.issue: The bullet says 'match or beat', which allows for parity. The source says LLMs 'consistently outperform' human-written summaries — a strictly stronger claim. 'Match or beat' understates the finding.
claim_verdicts.suggested_fix: Summarising text is a high-reliability task for current chat tools — LLM summaries consistently outperform human-written ones across news, dialogue, cross-lingual, and code tasks

claim_verdicts.slide: What AI assistants do well (green zone)
claim_verdicts.bullet: AI-generated summaries show equal or fewer hallucinations than human-written reference summaries
claim_verdicts.source_excerpt: Human-written reference summaries exhibit either an equal or higher number of hallucinations compared to GPT-4 summaries.

claim_verdicts.bullet: Arithmetic inside word problems degrades: logical error rates jump up to 14 percentage points as numbers get harder
claim_verdicts.source_excerpt: Our experiments with various models reveal a significant increase in logical error rates -- up to 14 percentage points -- as numerical complexity rises, demonstrating a general weakness in reasoning with out-of-distribution numerical values. Moreover, while models demonstrate high accuracy on standalone arithmetic tasks, their performance deteriorates substantially when computations are embedded within word problems.

claim_verdicts.bullet: Chat tools fabricate citations at high rates — even the best models invent 18-29% of references
claim_verdicts.source_excerpt: GhostCite benchmarks 13 LLMs across 40 domains (375K citations) with hallucination rates from 14% to 95%. ChatGPT-3.5 hallucinates 39.6% to 55% of citations in literature reviews, while GPT-4 still produces 18%-28.6% fabricated citations. Citation hallucination persists even when models have access to web search, with 3-13% of URLs fabricated in retrieval-augmented settings.
claim_verdicts.issue: The bullet rounds the GPT-4 upper bound of 28.6% up to 29%. The source states 18%-28.6%, not 18-29%. Additionally, the bullet frames GPT-4 as 'the best models' without stating that, while GPT-4 is the best-performing model cited, the source does not explicitly label it as the best overall.
claim_verdicts.suggested_fix: Chat tools fabricate citations at high rates — GPT-4 still invents 18–28.6% of references, and weaker models reach 55%

claim_verdicts.bullet: Sensitive-data leakage is the #2 risk in the OWASP Top 10 for AI apps (2025) — consumer tools may train on what you paste
claim_verdicts.source_excerpt: LLMs, particularly when embedded in applications, pose the risk of exposing sensitive data, proprietary algorithms, or confidential details through their outputs. This can result in unauthorized data access, privacy violations, and intellectual property breaches. Negligent inclusion of sensitive data in training sets results in disclosure through the model's outputs. Sensitive Information Disclosure rose from #6 (2023 edition) to #2 (2025 edition). Consumer-plan (Free, Plus) inputs may be used to train models unless users explicitly opt out via the privacy portal.

claim_verdicts.bullet: Samsung banned ChatGPT after engineers pasted source code, meeting notes, and chip-test data in three incidents within 20 days
claim_verdicts.source_excerpt: Samsung Electronics banned employee use of popular generative AI tools like ChatGPT after discovering staff uploaded sensitive code to the platform. Within less than 20 days of allowing ChatGPT access, Samsung experienced three separate incidents where engineers exposed confidential company information: 1. An employee copied source code from a faulty semiconductor database into ChatGPT and asked it to find a fix. 2. Another employee submitted an entire meeting transcript and asked the chatbot to create meeting minutes. 3. A third used ChatGPT to optimize a test sequence for identifying yield and defective chips.

claim_verdicts.bullet: Give the model three things: what you want to say, who you are writing to, and the tone you want
claim_verdicts.source_excerpt: Set role and tone in the system or developer message, not the user turn. Put the task instruction on line 1 and long context after.
claim_verdicts.issue: The source does not state or imply a 'three things' framework for email prompting. It discusses system-message placement of role/tone and instruction ordering — it says nothing about specifying 'who you are writing to' as an element to provide. The bullet's specific enumeration (intent, recipient, tone) is not found in or derivable from this source excerpt. This is a citation mismatch: the source does not support the claim.
claim_verdicts.suggested_fix: When prompting for email drafts, set the tone in the system message and put your task instruction first, with supporting context after — or retag this bullet to a source that actually enumerates the intent/recipient/tone framework

summary: 7 claims checked in this batch. 3 verified, 4 misrepresented, 0 unverified.
